3: The food sector is one of the most important manufacturing and economic sectors in Europe, contributing 14.5% of the total manufacturing turnover (European Comission, 2013). Additionally, to satisfy societal demand, an increasing demand for food has been observed. In parallel, the environmental impact associated with this sector has also experienced an increasing interest (González- Garacía et al., 2013a; Iribarren et al., 2011). The life cycle stages of food products (such as production, preservation and distribution) require large amounts of energy, which could be translated into

negative environmental impacts such as greenhouse gas (GHG) and acidifying emissions (Roy et al., 2008). In contrast, consumers in developed countries have started to demand high-quality food produced under more environmentally friendly conditions (Boer, 2002). In this context, there is an increased recognition and acceptance of the need to develop more sustainable systems within the food sector (Notarnicola et al., 2012). One of the most widely accepted international methods of quantifying the environmental impacts of food products is Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) (Roy et al., 2008). This is a standardized and holistic methodology that allows for the identification of the environmental consequences of the life cycle of a product by evaluating potential environmental impacts over its entire life cycle production chain (ISO 14040, 2006). LCA method has been chosen by numerous authors to conduct environmental assessment of a wide range of food products such as bread (Andersson et al., 1994),
* Corresponding author. CESAM, Department of Environment and Planning, University of Aveiro, 3810-193 Aveiro, Portugal. Tel.: +351 234370387.

tomato ketchup (Andersson et al., 1998), beer (Takamoto et al., 2004), dairy products (González-García et al., 2013a, 2013b; Hospido et al., 2003) and fish products (Hospido et al., 2006; Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2010).

Meat consumption is growing consistently worldwide as a result of its increasing demand by emerging countries (Ciolos, 2012). In Europe, the main types of meat - beef, veal, pork, chicken, goat and sheep - account for one quarter of the total value of agricultural production (European Commission, 2004). Previous LCA studies performed for beef (Cedeberg and Stadig, 2003; Beauchemin et al., 2010), pork (Reckmann et al., 2012) and poultry (Baumgartner et al., 2008; Bastianoni et al., 2010; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; Boggia et al., 2010; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008; Wiedema et al., 2008; Williams et al., 2006) are mainly related to the farm stages, while only a few include the slaughtering stage and/or packaging, dis- tribution and preparation (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007; Reckmann et al., 2012). In addition, different rearing scenarios (organic and conventional) have been compared from an environmental perspective in an attempt to define the best option (Boggia et al., 2010). Analysis from a cradle-to-grave perspective to obtain comprehensive information for the whole process (including the logistics, cooking and consumption stages) has been rarely considered (Roy et al., 2012; Wiedema et al., 2008).

Chicken meat has become one of the most extensively consumed food products in the world (Magdelaine et al., 2008). The highest consumption rates have been registered in the most industrialized countries, with Europe placed third in 2011 behind USA and China (Ministry of Agriculture Food and Environment of Spain, 2012). Apart from its low price, chicken is popular as it is an important source of proteins, vitamin B and minerals, and is low in saturated fats (Windhorst, 2006; FAO, 2012a). European chicken meat production represented 11% of total world production in 2011 (Ministry of Agriculture Food and Environment of Spain, 2012). In general, chicken production systems can be classified into two groups according to the farming scheme (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013): free-range chicken, which is reared outdoors with a purely vegetable diet, and broiler (or conventional) chicken, which is reared on farms with industrial feed as the base diet (Castellini et al., 2006).

This study focuses on environmental evaluation of broiler chicken production, from a cradle-to-slaughterhouse gate perspective, in Portugal, which ranks ninth in chicken production among European Union countries (Ministry of Agriculture Food and Environment of Spain, 2012). In addition, broiler chicken represents 75% of the total meat consumption in Portugal (30 kg of meat-cap-1.ye according to national statistics (INE, 2010). The results from this study are of potential interest for LCA prac- titioners and stakeholders in the meat sector as chicken meat OC- cupies the second position in the ranking of meat consumption in Europe (Magdelaine et al., 2008). Moreover, detailed inventory data has been reported which can help the LCA community focus on the quantification of environmental impacts derived from protein sources, which are basic ingredients in the human diet.

Previous LCA studies performed for beef (Cedeberg and Stadig, 2003; Beauchemin et al., 2010), pork (Reckmann et al., 2012) and poultry (Baumgartner et al., 2008; Bastianoni et al., 2010; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; Boggia et al., 2010; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008; Wiedema et al., 2008; Williams et al., 2006) are mainly related to the farm stages, while only a few include the slaughtering stage and/or packaging, dis- tribution and preparation (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007; Reckmann et al., 2012). In addition, different rearing scenarios (organic and conventional) have been compared from an environmental perspective in an attempt to define the best option (Boggia et al., 2010). Analysis from a cradle-to-grave perspective to obtain comprehensive information for the whole process (including the logistics, cooking and consumption stages) has been rarely considered (Roy et al., 2012; Wiedema et al., 2008).

Chicken meat has become one of the most extensively consumed food products in the world (Magdelaine et al., 2008). The highest consumption rates have been registered in the most industrialized countries, with Europe placed third in 2011 behind USA and China (Ministry of Agriculture Food and Environment of Spain, 2012). Apart from its low price, chicken is popular as it is an important source of proteins, vitamin B and minerals, and is low in saturated fats (Windhorst, 2006; FAO, 2012a). European chicken meat production represented 11% of total world production in 2011 (Ministry of Agriculture Food and Environment of Spain, 2012). In general, chicken production systems can be classified into two groups according to the farming scheme (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013): free-range chicken, which is reared outdoors with a purely vegetable diet, and broiler (or conventional) chicken, which is reared on farms with industrial feed as the base diet (Castellini et al., 2006).

This study focuses on environmental evaluation of broiler chicken production, from a cradle-to-slaughterhouse gate perspective, in Portugal, which ranks ninth in chicken production among European Union countries (Ministry of Agriculture Food and Environment of Spain, 2012). In addition, broiler chicken represents 75% of the total meat consumption in Portugal (30 kg of meat-cap-1.ye according to national statistics (INE, 2010). The results from this study are of potential interest for LCA prac- titioners and stakeholders in the meat sector as chicken meat OC- cupies the second position in the ranking of meat consumption in Europe (Magdelaine et al., 2008). Moreover, detailed inventory data has been reported which can help the LCA community focus on the quantification of environmental impacts derived from protein sources, which are basic ingredients in the human diet.
9: The main function of the system under study is to satisfy the food requirements for chicken in an average Portuguese family during one meal. To do that, this study aims to identify and evaluate the environmental impacts throughout the life cycle of broiler chicken through a cradle-to-gate perspective, from extraction of
raw materials to the slaughterhouse gate. Moreover, the study proposes different improvement alternatives for the farm to reduce the environmental burdens associated with this activity.

The functional unit, 1.2 kg of broiler chicken meat ready to be distributed to the point of sale, was chosen because a) it is the amount of raw chicken that contains the recommended daily pro- tein supply for an average family (four people) (CDC, 2012), and b) it is the average weight of a broiler chicken (excluding packaging) available for consumption in Portuguese markets.
10: <header>2.2. Description of the system under study and system boundaries </header>

The production system under study encompassed all activities related to the chicken farm and the slaughterhouse according to a cradle-to-gate analysis (Fig. 1). In this way, the product (broiler chicken) could be monitored until it was packaged and ready for distribution to wholesales.
Thus, the production system in this study was divided in two main subsystems: the chicken farm and the slaughterhouse.

Chicken farm: Activities carried out directly in this subsystem start with the reception of chicks at the farm gate. Chicks are transferred to a production pen for a fattening period of 34 days. Chickens are housed in poultry beds, which are commonly composed of a mixture of materials such as sawdust, bark, wood shavings and/or chips. Production of these bedding materials as well as their distribution to the chicken farm was considered within the subsystem boundaries.

As mentioned, chickens are fed for 34 days with a ratio of 85 g of chicken-1 The production of chicken feed was included within the subsystem boundaries (Fig. 1). The feed is mainly composed of maize, wheat, soybean cake, soybean oil, monocalcium phosphate, protein concentrate and fats.
Throughout the growth period, vaccines and antibiotics are administered. The production and use of these pharmaceuticals were excluded from the assessment due to the lack of information.

Cleaning activities at the chicken farm involve not only water but also chemicals. The production and use of cleaning agents (detergents and disinfectants) were not considered within the system boundaries in agreement with other studies (Castanheira et al., 2010; Hospido et al., 2003), which state that the environ- mental burdens derived from the production of these substances do not represent a significant percentage of the global environ- mental profile of the entire system. Wastewater generated from cleaning activities and farm-related uses is treated in a small wastewater treatment plant (WWTP), which is located on the chicken farm.

Manure produced in the farm is an important source of nutri- ents. The wasted poultry beds and manure (poultry litter) are spread over agricultural land. This organic waste management (including handling, storage and application) cause emissions such as NH3, N20, CH4, NO3 and PO43. Its application as an organic fer- tilizer avoids the production of an equivalent amount of mineral fertilizers (N, P and K), which has also been considered within this subsystem boundaries.

According to several studies in which the environmental im- pacts of farm-related activities were identified (e.g., Castanheira et al., 2010; Hospido et al., 2003), feed production is one of the main factors responsible for environmental damage. Thus, special attention was paid to feed production-related activities. Feed pro- duction (Fig. 1) involves the production of different raw materials (primarily cereals), which are stored separately in silos. First, the raw materials are ground to reduce their size, before being measured, weighed and mixed. Thereafter, the product is granu- lated using steam produced in boilers and stored in bulk bags or silos to be delivered to the farm gate. Fungicides are also required to

Fig. 1. System boundaries and flow chart of the broiler chicken production system under assessment: solid boxes correspond to processes included in this study. Dotted boxes correspond to processes excluded from assessment.

maintain adequate disinfection conditions. Their production and use was included within the system boundaries. In the feed production line, a large amount of water taken from the public network is required for cleaning activities and steam production. The wastewater produced is treated in a compact WWTP and this treatment is included as part of the feed production process. The WWTP consists of three steps: pre-treatment, ho- mogenization and decantation. It is important to note that the wastewater generated during steam production could contain fuel residues and, for that reason, it is passed through a separation tank with a double decantation step before being sent to the WWTP. Slaughterhouse: First, chickens are transported from the chicken farm to the slaughterhouse in trucks. Once at the slaughterhouse, chickens are killed by an electric shock, scalded with boiling water and the feathers and viscera are removed. Next, cleaned chickens are sent to a cooling tunnel and then classified by weight. Finally, whole chickens are packaged in polystyrene trays and LDPE film. Both the production of packaging materials and their distribution to the slaughterhouse were also considered within the subsystem boundaries. Organic residues from this subsystem such as feathers,

heads, feet and viscera are sent to a receiving tank followed by a solid/liquid separator. The liquid stream, with a high content of organic matter, is sent to the WWTP, while the solid stream is stored in a tank, awaiting further collection by a licensed solid waste manager. The most important destination of this solid stream is the production of components to pet food. Activities related to solid-stream processing were excluded from the system bound- aries because they are located outside of the slaughterhouse (sys- tem boundaries) and have valorization, i.e. they can assume the status of co-production, in accordance with the methodology pro- posed by Clift et al. (2000). As in the chicken farm, water taken from the public supply network is required for different activities in the slaughterhouse (cleaning activities and the production itself). Once again, the production of the cleaning agents required during cleaning activ- ities was considered to be outside the subsystem boundaries due to a lack of data. The wastewater produced is treated in the WWTP in the slaughterhouse, which comprises physico-chemical and bio- logical treatments. The production of capital goods and in- frastructures was excluded from the assessment.

The WWTP consists of three steps: pre-treatment, ho- mogenization and decantation. It is important to note that the wastewater generated during steam production could contain fuel residues and, for that reason, it is passed through a separation tank with a double decantation step before being sent to the WWTP. Slaughterhouse: First, chickens are transported from the chicken farm to the slaughterhouse in trucks. Once at the slaughterhouse, chickens are killed by an electric shock, scalded with boiling water and the feathers and viscera are removed. Next, cleaned chickens are sent to a cooling tunnel and then classified by weight. Finally, whole chickens are packaged in polystyrene trays and LDPE film. Both the production of packaging materials and their distribution to the slaughterhouse were also considered within the subsystem boundaries. Organic residues from this subsystem such as feathers,

heads, feet and viscera are sent to a receiving tank followed by a solid/liquid separator. The liquid stream, with a high content of organic matter, is sent to the WWTP, while the solid stream is stored in a tank, awaiting further collection by a licensed solid waste manager. The most important destination of this solid stream is the production of components to pet food. Activities related to solid-stream processing were excluded from the system bound- aries because they are located outside of the slaughterhouse (sys- tem boundaries) and have valorization, i.e. they can assume the status of co-production, in accordance with the methodology pro- posed by Clift et al. (2000). As in the chicken farm, water taken from the public supply network is required for different activities in the slaughterhouse (cleaning activities and the production itself). Once again, the production of the cleaning agents required during cleaning activ- ities was considered to be outside the subsystem boundaries due to a lack of data. The wastewater produced is treated in the WWTP in the slaughterhouse, which comprises physico-chemical and bio- logical treatments. The production of capital goods and in- frastructures was excluded from the assessment.
16: <header>2.3. Allocation approach </header>

Allocation, an important issue in LCA studies, consists of parti- tioning the input and/or output flows of a process for the product system under study. It is required for multi-functional processes and the selection of an allocation approach can have a significant effect on the results. In this study, allocation was not considered. In the chicken farm, the unique product is live chickens. The waste, beds and manure do not provide any revenue for the farmers and their disposal has to be paid for.

Concerning the slaughterhouse, the main product is packaged broiler chickens ready for consumption. Organic residues produced in the slaughterhouse are commonly processed as an element of pet food. In Portugal these types of residue have no commercial value, despite their use in the production of pet food. In countries where these residues have commercial value, an economic allocation should be carried out.
17: <header>2.4. Inventory analysis </header>

In LCA studies, it is important to consider real data in order to obtain representative and relevant environmental results. In this study, life cycle inventory (LCI) data for both subsystems was collected through surveys, interviews and visits to the farm and slaughterhouse of a broiler-chicken company in Portugal. All the primary data corresponded to 2010.

The chicken farm was assessed and inventoried in detail to gather representative data for Portuguese production as one of the most important farms in terms of broiler chicken production vol- ume was studied (Table 1). Animal production systems (in this case broiler chicken) involve emissions into the environment and the consumption of resources. The effects of these emissions and consumptions in the environ- ment can be illustrated in terms of different impact categories: acidification potential (AP), eutrophication potential (EP), global warming potential (GWP) and photochemical oxidant formation potential (POFP) are considerably affected by derived emissions such as CO2, CH4 and N20. In addition, special attention is paid to abiotic depletion potential (ADP) and the cumulative non- renewable fossil and nuclear energy demand (CED) in order to identify the depletion of resources and energy requirements throughout the life cycle (Reckman et al., 2012). Thus, these impact categories have been considered for assessment in this study for several reasons:

<list>i) LCA indicators for all of them are very well established (although there are still methodological differences which can hinder detailed comparisons of results from different researchers); 
ii) impacts derived from livestock production systems are very related with these impact categories (mainly GWP, AP and EP due to methane, ammonia, nitrate and nitrous oxide derived from manure production, handling and management) and,</list>

iii) these impact categories are the most widely used in envi- ronmental studies regardless of the animal production sys- tem which can facilitate the comparisons (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Cedeberg and Stadig, 2003; Reckman et al., 2012).

Characterization factors developed by the Institute of Environ- mental Sciences of Leiden University (CML 2001 method) have been considered for ADP, AP, EP, GWP and POFP (Guinée et al., 2001). The energy analysis (CED) was performed according to the method proposed by Hischier et al. (2009). The SimaPro 7.3.2 software was used to process the inventories (PRé Consultants, 2012).

4: Production of the broiler chicken on the farm contributed most to environmental impacts in all categories, with significant con- tributions to EP, AP, POFP and GWP (99%, 98%, 99% and 93% of total contributing emissions, respectively) (Table 5).

Therefore, special attention should be paid to the chicken farm in order to identify the main environmental and energetic hotspots the contributions of the involved processes in both sub- systems are detailed below.
5: The contributions of processes carried out on the chicken farm were dominated by feed production and on-farm emissions due to farm activities (Fig. 2).

The contribution of feed production to environmental impacts derived from the chicken farm was especially high, ranging from 23% (in AP) to 79% (in POFP). As reported by several studies, feed production is the process most responsible for environmental im- pacts associated with farm-related activities (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; Castanheira et al., 2010; Hospido et al., 2003; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008). Feed production can be confirmed as one of the most important hotspots in the environ- mental impacts and energy use of chicken farms (Fig. 2). Therefore, special attention was paid to it. The production of feed ingredients was the main process responsible for the environmental impacts and energy use of feed production (Fig. 3), especially for EP (98%), CED (96%) and POFP (95%). The contributions to these categories are related to the production and use of fertilizers to generate feed ingredients. Of the specific feed ingredients (Table 2), soybeans (meal and oil) were responsible for the highest contributions in all categories (up to 87%) due to its cropping stage. Soybeans are the main protein source in chicken feed. From these soybeans, soybean meal and soybean oil are produced, and soybean meal is used most often as a protein source. In fact, in agreement with Bengtson et al. (2013) and Tongpool et al. (2012), soybean meal is an ingredient that has relatively high impacts.

Electricity and heat production contributed to environmental impacts (Fig. 3). The impact categories mainly affected by com- bustion emissions (SO2, CO2, CO) derived from heat production were AP (22%) and GWP (21%). The production of electricity for milling and granulation of feed contributes to ADP (16%) due to its dependence on fossil fuels.

On-farm emissions mainly involved NH3, N20 and CH4 emis- sions from organic waste (manure and waste poultry beds) man- agement (handling, storage and application in fields). Thus, this contributing factor resulted in remarkable contributions in cate- gories such as AP (69%), EP (34%), GWP (11%) and POFP (13%), as can be seen in Fig. 2. The production of feed ingredients was the main process responsible for the environmental impacts and energy use of feed production (Fig. 3), especially for EP (98%), CED (96%) and POFP (95%). The contributions to these categories are related to the production and use of fertilizers to generate feed ingredients. Of the specific feed ingredients (Table 2), soybeans (meal and oil) were responsible for the highest contributions in all categories (up to 87%) due to its cropping stage. Soybeans are the main protein source in chicken feed. From these soybeans, soybean meal and soybean oil are produced, and soybean meal is used most often as a protein source. In fact, in agreement with Bengtson et al. (2013) and Tongpool et al. (2012), soybean meal is an ingredient that has relatively high impacts.

Electricity and heat production contributed to environmental impacts (Fig. 3). The impact categories mainly affected by com- bustion emissions (SO2, CO2, CO) derived from heat production were AP (22%) and GWP (21%). The production of electricity for milling and granulation of feed contributes to ADP (16%) due to its dependence on fossil fuels.

The slaughterhouse has a significant electricity demand due to activities such as machinery operation, refrigeration, lighting, ventilation and WWTP operation. However, special attention must be paid to refrigeration, which is necessary to maintain the required characteristics of meat, and the production of the electric shock, which is necessary to kill the chickens. Electricity production is a hotspot in all the categories examined, contributing between 31% (in CED) and 61% (in AP).
Heat requirements are also high in the slaughterhouse (e.g. scalding). Heat production in this subsystem contributed 25% in CED and 31% in GWP.

This is also due to organic waste management (mostly NH3 and CH4 emissions from handling and storage), which contributes to EP and AP, and poultry bedding production, which contributes mainly to GWP, ADP and CED. Other broiler-chicken studies have reported similar results (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008). A common result in studies including the slaughtering stage (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007) is the low contribution of the slaughterhouse stage to the total impacts. However, special attention must be paid to electricity consumption in the slaughterhouse, which is particularly high for the WWTP, refrigeration, and the production of the electric shock.

Including slaughterhouse activities, the value of GWP in the present study is around 2.46 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per carcass weight. Excluding them, GWP is around 1.62 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per live weight. GWP results reported in the literature (Baumgartner et al., 2008; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008; Wiedema et al., 2008; Williams et al., 2006) vary significantly (Table 6). The differences in these results depend mainly on the country in which the study was performed, data quality (the use of primary or secondary data), co-products/ waste handling, consideration of hatchery chicks and chicken feed composition. The GWP of broiler chicken production in Portugal is below the average of these results.

When focussing only on chicken farm activities (Baumgartner et al., 2008; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008; Williams et al., 2006), GWP ranges from 1.48 to 2.61 kg CO2 eq kg- per live weight. Studies from Bengtsson and Seddon (2013), Katajajuuri (2007), Pelletier (2008) and Williams et al. (2006) carried out in very different countries have similar GWPs (2.00-2.61 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per live weight), although they all have different system boundaries. One of the main factors responsible for these slight differences in GWP is the consideration of chicken bed production in Bengtsson and Seddon (2013) and Williams et al. (2006) (as in our study). More- over, within the farm activities, Pelletier (2008) considered the use of poultry litter to displace fertilizer production, the poultry fat and the by-product meal production from waste, as well as the avoid- ance of meal production.

Da Silva et al. (2012) reported a 1.48 and 2.19 kg CO2 eq kg-1 live weight for chicken production in Brazil and France, respectively. The main factor responsible for this different result is related to the feed composition and corresponding production, as well as organic waste management.
The GWP in Baumgartner et al. (2008) is considerably higher (almost double) than our results (Table 6). This study considered

the inclusion of infrastructure production and maintenance within the system boundaries, although the contribution is almost negli- gible in this impact category. Differences in data quality and farming practices (e.g., feed composition and consumption, heat requirements and heating sources) could be the reason for the large difference in GHG emissions.

Weidema et al. (2008) reported results corresponding to an average value for Europe. It is the most complete study as the distribution to retailers and consumption was included (3.60 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per carcass weight). However, it is not possible to identify GHG emissions from the individual processes involved due to the lack of information in the report, meaning it could not be compared with our system. However, the contribution of chicken farm related activities to categories such as GWP, AP and EP are significant according to Weidema et al. (2008). According to a re- view of the poultry sector by Bengtsson and Seddon (2013), the GHG emission rate ranges from 2.00 to 5.48 kg CO2 eq kg` per live weight. Again, our result is below this range.

Environmental impacts have been quantified not only for chicken meat production but also for other types of meat such as pork and beef (Reckman et al., 2012; Beauchemin et al., 2010). Quantifying the environmental impacts of food products is important due to society's interest in consuming more environ- mentally friendly produce. Meat is an important source of proteins for humans and differs in protein content depending on the type of meat. Therefore, we compared the total GHG emissions of the most commonly consumed types of meat (i.e., chicken, pork and beef) (European Commission, 2004) and one of the most consumed types of fish in Portugal (sardines) (Cardoso et al., 2013). The consider- ation of fish as a source of protein is important in Portugal since it has a higher average annual per capita seafood consumption than all but one other European country (Cardoso et al., 2013).

To do so, an equivalent functional unit was required. We considered 168.4 g of protein, which corresponds to the amount of protein supplied by one Portuguese broiler chicken with an average weight of 1.20 kg (14.04 g protein per 100 g of carcass weight). Therefore, 1.50 kg of pork, 1.02 kg of beef and 0.94 kg of fresh sar- dines were considered for comparison (11.2 g protein per 100 g of pork carcass weight, 16.5 protein per 100 g of beef carcass weight and 18.0 protein per 100 g of fresh weight sardines1 (Bandarra et al., 2004; FAO, 2012b).
Identical system boundaries were taken into account for the three meat production systems studied (i.e., cradle to

slaughterhouse gate), excluding the packaging process. Concerning the fish, system boundaries included all fishery activities up to the sardine landing process for sale at Portuguese fish auctions (Almeida et al., 2013; Belo et al., 2013).

Reckman et al. (2012) reported a GWP of 3.6 kg CO2 eq per kg of pork carcass weight, which is slightly higher than our result for chicken (2.46 kg CO2 eq, Table 6). An array of GHG emissions was found for beef production systems, ranging from 17 to 37 kg CO2 eq per kg of beef carcass weight (Cedeberg and Stadig, 2003; Beauchemin et al., 2010). Therefore, these GWP values corre- spond to 5.4 and 22.4 kg CO2 eq per functional unit (168.4 g protein) for pork and beef, respectively. Concerning sardines, Almeida et al. (2013) and Belo et al. (2013) reported a value of GWP ranging from 0.27 to 0.47 kg CO2 eq per kg of fresh sardines, resulting in an average of 0.35 kg CO2 eq per functional unit. Thus, based on GWP alone, the production of broiler chicken should be preferable to that of pork or beef since it emits up to 44% and 86% fewer GHGs throughout the production life cycle. This statement agrees with other studies (Roy et al., 2008; Wiedema et al., 2008). In fact, Roy et al. (2008) reported that beef is the lowest envi- ronmentally friendly meat since the greater feed conversion ratio (the mass of the feed consumed divided by the gain in body mass) of cows results in higher emissions from feed production. On the contrary, sardines should be preferable compared with broiler chicken as a source of proteins in a meal since the GWP related with the sardine production system should be around 88% lower than chicken. However, although the environmental profile should be better, there are some protocols that must be taken into account for fishery products. In contrast to meat, there are pol- icies in place to limit the number of vessels and days at sea in addition to quota restrictions limiting the total allowable catches (TACs). This action, designed to maintain the fishing stock levels at desirable abundance rates, was imposed due to over- exploitation in some fisheries (Vázquez-Rowe and Tyedmers, 2013).

The results from this study can support policy makers and in- dustries in order to identify improvement options focussed on the reduction of the environmental profiles of food production sys- items. Moreover, it could enable consumers to make informed purchasing decisions when confronted with various alternative protein source products in terms of the environmental perfor- mance of those products.

environmental profile ranging from 84% to 99%. A common result in studies including the slaughtering stage (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007) is the low contribution of the slaughterhouse stage to the total impacts. However, special attention must be paid to electricity consumption in the slaughterhouse, which is particularly high for the WWTP, refrigeration, and the production of the electric shock.

Including slaughterhouse activities, the value of GWP in the present study is around 2.46 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per carcass weight. Excluding them, GWP is around 1.62 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per live weight. GWP results reported in the literature (Baumgartner et al., 2008; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008; Wiedema et al., 2008; Williams et al., 2006) vary significantly (Table 6). The differences in these results depend mainly on the country in which the study was performed, data quality (the use of primary or secondary data), co-products/ waste handling, consideration of hatchery chicks and chicken feed composition. The GWP of broiler chicken production in Portugal is below the average of these results.

When focussing only on chicken farm activities (Baumgartner et al., 2008; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008; Williams et al., 2006), GWP ranges from 1.48 to 2.61 kg CO2 eq kg- per live weight. Studies from Bengtsson and Seddon (2013), Katajajuuri (2007), Pelletier (2008) and Williams et al. (2006) carried out in very different countries have similar GWPs (2.00-2.61 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per live weight), although they all have different system boundaries. One of the main factors responsible for these slight differences in GWP is the consideration of chicken bed production in Bengtsson and Seddon (2013) and Williams et al. (2006) (as in our study). More- over, within the farm activities, Pelletier (2008) considered the use of poultry litter to displace fertilizer production, the poultry fat and the by-product meal production from waste, as well as the avoid- ance of meal production.

Da Silva et al. (2012) reported a 1.48 and 2.19 kg CO2 eq kg-1 live weight for chicken production in Brazil and France, respectively. The main factor responsible for this different result is related to the feed composition and corresponding production, as well as organic waste management.
The GWP in Baumgartner et al. (2008) is considerably higher (almost double) than our results (Table 6). This study considered

the inclusion of infrastructure production and maintenance within the system boundaries, although the contribution is almost negli- gible in this impact category. Differences in data quality and farming practices (e.g., feed composition and consumption, heat requirements and heating sources) could be the reason for the large difference in GHG emissions.

Weidema et al. (2008) reported results corresponding to an average value for Europe. It is the most complete study as the distribution to retailers and consumption was included (3.60 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per carcass weight). However, it is not possible to identify GHG emissions from the individual processes involved due to the lack of information in the report, meaning it could not be compared with our system. However, the contribution of chicken farm related activities to categories such as GWP, AP and EP are significant according to Weidema et al. (2008). According to a re- view of the poultry sector by Bengtsson and Seddon (2013), the GHG emission rate ranges from 2.00 to 5.48 kg CO2 eq kg` per live weight. Again, our result is below this range.

Environmental impacts have been quantified not only for chicken meat production but also for other types of meat such as pork and beef (Reckman et al., 2012; Beauchemin et al., 2010). Quantifying the environmental impacts of food products is important due to society's interest in consuming more environ- mentally friendly produce. Meat is an important source of proteins for humans and differs in protein content depending on the type of meat. Therefore, we compared the total GHG emissions of the most commonly consumed types of meat (i.e., chicken, pork and beef) (European Commission, 2004) and one of the most consumed types of fish in Portugal (sardines) (Cardoso et al., 2013). The consider- ation of fish as a source of protein is important in Portugal since it has a higher average annual per capita seafood consumption than all but one other European country (Cardoso et al., 2013).

To do so, an equivalent functional unit was required. We considered 168.4 g of protein, which corresponds to the amount of protein supplied by one Portuguese broiler chicken with an average weight of 1.20 kg (14.04 g protein per 100 g of carcass weight). Therefore, 1.50 kg of pork, 1.02 kg of beef and 0.94 kg of fresh sar- dines were considered for comparison (11.2 g protein per 100 g of pork carcass weight, 16.5 protein per 100 g of beef carcass weight and 18.0 protein per 100 g of fresh weight sardines1 (Bandarra et al., 2004; FAO, 2012b).
Identical system boundaries were taken into account for the three meat production systems studied (i.e., cradle to

slaughterhouse gate), excluding the packaging process. Concerning the fish, system boundaries included all fishery activities up to the sardine landing process for sale at Portuguese fish auctions (Almeida et al., 2013; Belo et al., 2013).

Reckman et al. (2012) reported a GWP of 3.6 kg CO2 eq per kg of pork carcass weight, which is slightly higher than our result for chicken (2.46 kg CO2 eq, Table 6). An array of GHG emissions was found for beef production systems, ranging from 17 to 37 kg CO2 eq per kg of beef carcass weight (Cedeberg and Stadig, 2003; Beauchemin et al., 2010). Therefore, these GWP values corre- spond to 5.4 and 22.4 kg CO2 eq per functional unit (168.4 g protein) for pork and beef, respectively. Concerning sardines, Almeida et al. (2013) and Belo et al. (2013) reported a value of GWP ranging from 0.27 to 0.47 kg CO2 eq per kg of fresh sardines, resulting in an average of 0.35 kg CO2 eq per functional unit. Thus, based on GWP alone, the production of broiler chicken should be preferable to that of pork or beef since it emits up to 44% and 86% fewer GHGs throughout the production life cycle. This statement agrees with other studies (Roy et al., 2008; Wiedema et al., 2008). In fact, Roy et al. (2008) reported that beef is the lowest envi- ronmentally friendly meat since the greater feed conversion ratio (the mass of the feed consumed divided by the gain in body mass) of cows results in higher emissions from feed production. On the contrary, sardines should be preferable compared with broiler chicken as a source of proteins in a meal since the GWP related with the sardine production system should be around 88% lower than chicken. However, although the environmental profile should be better, there are some protocols that must be taken into account for fishery products. In contrast to meat, there are pol- icies in place to limit the number of vessels and days at sea in addition to quota restrictions limiting the total allowable catches (TACs). This action, designed to maintain the fishing stock levels at desirable abundance rates, was imposed due to over- exploitation in some fisheries (Vázquez-Rowe and Tyedmers, 2013).

The results from this study can support policy makers and in- dustries in order to identify improvement options focussed on the reduction of the environmental profiles of food production sys- items. Moreover, it could enable consumers to make informed purchasing decisions when confronted with various alternative protein source products in terms of the environmental perfor- mance of those products.

environmental profile ranging from 84% to 99%. This is also due to organic waste management (mostly NH3 and CH4 emissions from handling and storage), which contributes to EP and AP, and poultry bedding production, which contributes mainly to GWP, ADP and CED. Other broiler-chicken studies have reported similar results (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008). A common result in studies including the slaughtering stage (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007) is the low contribution of the slaughterhouse stage to the total impacts. However, special attention must be paid to electricity consumption in the slaughterhouse, which is particularly high for the WWTP, refrigeration, and the production of the electric shock.

Including slaughterhouse activities, the value of GWP in the present study is around 2.46 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per carcass weight. Excluding them, GWP is around 1.62 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per live weight. GWP results reported in the literature (Baumgartner et al., 2008; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008; Wiedema et al., 2008; Williams et al., 2006) vary significantly (Table 6). The differences in these results depend mainly on the country in which the study was performed, data quality (the use of primary or secondary data), co-products/ waste handling, consideration of hatchery chicks and chicken feed composition. The GWP of broiler chicken production in Portugal is below the average of these results.

When focussing only on chicken farm activities (Baumgartner et al., 2008; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008; Williams et al., 2006), GWP ranges from 1.48 to 2.61 kg CO2 eq kg- per live weight. Studies from Bengtsson and Seddon (2013), Katajajuuri (2007), Pelletier (2008) and Williams et al. (2006) carried out in very different countries have similar GWPs (2.00-2.61 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per live weight), although they all have different system boundaries. One of the main factors responsible for these slight differences in GWP is the consideration of chicken bed production in Bengtsson and Seddon (2013) and Williams et al. (2006) (as in our study). More- over, within the farm activities, Pelletier (2008) considered the use of poultry litter to displace fertilizer production, the poultry fat and the by-product meal production from waste, as well as the avoid- ance of meal production.

Da Silva et al. (2012) reported a 1.48 and 2.19 kg CO2 eq kg-1 live weight for chicken production in Brazil and France, respectively. The main factor responsible for this different result is related to the feed composition and corresponding production, as well as organic waste management.
The GWP in Baumgartner et al. (2008) is considerably higher (almost double) than our results (Table 6). This study considered

the inclusion of infrastructure production and maintenance within the system boundaries, although the contribution is almost negli- gible in this impact category. Differences in data quality and farming practices (e.g., feed composition and consumption, heat requirements and heating sources) could be the reason for the large difference in GHG emissions.

Weidema et al. (2008) reported results corresponding to an average value for Europe. It is the most complete study as the distribution to retailers and consumption was included (3.60 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per carcass weight). However, it is not possible to identify GHG emissions from the individual processes involved due to the lack of information in the report, meaning it could not be compared with our system. However, the contribution of chicken farm related activities to categories such as GWP, AP and EP are significant according to Weidema et al. (2008). According to a re- view of the poultry sector by Bengtsson and Seddon (2013), the GHG emission rate ranges from 2.00 to 5.48 kg CO2 eq kg` per live weight. Again, our result is below this range.

Environmental impacts have been quantified not only for chicken meat production but also for other types of meat such as pork and beef (Reckman et al., 2012; Beauchemin et al., 2010). Quantifying the environmental impacts of food products is important due to society's interest in consuming more environ- mentally friendly produce. Meat is an important source of proteins for humans and differs in protein content depending on the type of meat. Therefore, we compared the total GHG emissions of the most commonly consumed types of meat (i.e., chicken, pork and beef) (European Commission, 2004) and one of the most consumed types of fish in Portugal (sardines) (Cardoso et al., 2013). The consider- ation of fish as a source of protein is important in Portugal since it has a higher average annual per capita seafood consumption than all but one other European country (Cardoso et al., 2013).

To do so, an equivalent functional unit was required. We considered 168.4 g of protein, which corresponds to the amount of protein supplied by one Portuguese broiler chicken with an average weight of 1.20 kg (14.04 g protein per 100 g of carcass weight). Therefore, 1.50 kg of pork, 1.02 kg of beef and 0.94 kg of fresh sar- dines were considered for comparison (11.2 g protein per 100 g of pork carcass weight, 16.5 protein per 100 g of beef carcass weight and 18.0 protein per 100 g of fresh weight sardines1 (Bandarra et al., 2004; FAO, 2012b).
Identical system boundaries were taken into account for the three meat production systems studied (i.e., cradle to

slaughterhouse gate), excluding the packaging process. Concerning the fish, system boundaries included all fishery activities up to the sardine landing process for sale at Portuguese fish auctions (Almeida et al., 2013; Belo et al., 2013).

Reckman et al. (2012) reported a GWP of 3.6 kg CO2 eq per kg of pork carcass weight, which is slightly higher than our result for chicken (2.46 kg CO2 eq, Table 6). An array of GHG emissions was found for beef production systems, ranging from 17 to 37 kg CO2 eq per kg of beef carcass weight (Cedeberg and Stadig, 2003; Beauchemin et al., 2010). Therefore, these GWP values corre- spond to 5.4 and 22.4 kg CO2 eq per functional unit (168.4 g protein) for pork and beef, respectively. Concerning sardines, Almeida et al. (2013) and Belo et al. (2013) reported a value of GWP ranging from 0.27 to 0.47 kg CO2 eq per kg of fresh sardines, resulting in an average of 0.35 kg CO2 eq per functional unit. Thus, based on GWP alone, the production of broiler chicken should be preferable to that of pork or beef since it emits up to 44% and 86% fewer GHGs throughout the production life cycle. This statement agrees with other studies (Roy et al., 2008; Wiedema et al., 2008). In fact, Roy et al. (2008) reported that beef is the lowest envi- ronmentally friendly meat since the greater feed conversion ratio (the mass of the feed consumed divided by the gain in body mass) of cows results in higher emissions from feed production. On the contrary, sardines should be preferable compared with broiler chicken as a source of proteins in a meal since the GWP related with the sardine production system should be around 88% lower than chicken. However, although the environmental profile should be better, there are some protocols that must be taken into account for fishery products. In contrast to meat, there are pol- icies in place to limit the number of vessels and days at sea in addition to quota restrictions limiting the total allowable catches (TACs). This action, designed to maintain the fishing stock levels at desirable abundance rates, was imposed due to over- exploitation in some fisheries (Vázquez-Rowe and Tyedmers, 2013).

The results from this study can support policy makers and in- dustries in order to identify improvement options focussed on the reduction of the environmental profiles of food production sys- items. Moreover, it could enable consumers to make informed purchasing decisions when confronted with various alternative protein source products in terms of the environmental perfor- mance of those products.

environmental profile ranging from 84% to 99%. A common result in studies including the slaughtering stage (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007) is the low contribution of the slaughterhouse stage to the total impacts. However, special attention must be paid to electricity consumption in the slaughterhouse, which is particularly high for the WWTP, refrigeration, and the production of the electric shock.

Including slaughterhouse activities, the value of GWP in the present study is around 2.46 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per carcass weight. Excluding them, GWP is around 1.62 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per live weight. GWP results reported in the literature (Baumgartner et al., 2008; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008; Wiedema et al., 2008; Williams et al., 2006) vary significantly (Table 6). The differences in these results depend mainly on the country in which the study was performed, data quality (the use of primary or secondary data), co-products/ waste handling, consideration of hatchery chicks and chicken feed composition. The GWP of broiler chicken production in Portugal is below the average of these results.

When focussing only on chicken farm activities (Baumgartner et al., 2008; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008; Williams et al., 2006), GWP ranges from 1.48 to 2.61 kg CO2 eq kg- per live weight. Studies from Bengtsson and Seddon (2013), Katajajuuri (2007), Pelletier (2008) and Williams et al. (2006) carried out in very different countries have similar GWPs (2.00-2.61 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per live weight), although they all have different system boundaries. One of the main factors responsible for these slight differences in GWP is the consideration of chicken bed production in Bengtsson and Seddon (2013) and Williams et al. (2006) (as in our study). More- over, within the farm activities, Pelletier (2008) considered the use of poultry litter to displace fertilizer production, the poultry fat and the by-product meal production from waste, as well as the avoid- ance of meal production.

Da Silva et al. (2012) reported a 1.48 and 2.19 kg CO2 eq kg-1 live weight for chicken production in Brazil and France, respectively. The main factor responsible for this different result is related to the feed composition and corresponding production, as well as organic waste management.
The GWP in Baumgartner et al. (2008) is considerably higher (almost double) than our results (Table 6). This study considered

the inclusion of infrastructure production and maintenance within the system boundaries, although the contribution is almost negli- gible in this impact category. Differences in data quality and farming practices (e.g., feed composition and consumption, heat requirements and heating sources) could be the reason for the large difference in GHG emissions.

Weidema et al. (2008) reported results corresponding to an average value for Europe. It is the most complete study as the distribution to retailers and consumption was included (3.60 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per carcass weight). However, it is not possible to identify GHG emissions from the individual processes involved due to the lack of information in the report, meaning it could not be compared with our system. However, the contribution of chicken farm related activities to categories such as GWP, AP and EP are significant according to Weidema et al. (2008). According to a re- view of the poultry sector by Bengtsson and Seddon (2013), the GHG emission rate ranges from 2.00 to 5.48 kg CO2 eq kg` per live weight. Again, our result is below this range.

Environmental impacts have been quantified not only for chicken meat production but also for other types of meat such as pork and beef (Reckman et al., 2012; Beauchemin et al., 2010). Quantifying the environmental impacts of food products is important due to society's interest in consuming more environ- mentally friendly produce. Meat is an important source of proteins for humans and differs in protein content depending on the type of meat. Therefore, we compared the total GHG emissions of the most commonly consumed types of meat (i.e., chicken, pork and beef) (European Commission, 2004) and one of the most consumed types of fish in Portugal (sardines) (Cardoso et al., 2013). The consider- ation of fish as a source of protein is important in Portugal since it has a higher average annual per capita seafood consumption than all but one other European country (Cardoso et al., 2013).

To do so, an equivalent functional unit was required. We considered 168.4 g of protein, which corresponds to the amount of protein supplied by one Portuguese broiler chicken with an average weight of 1.20 kg (14.04 g protein per 100 g of carcass weight). Therefore, 1.50 kg of pork, 1.02 kg of beef and 0.94 kg of fresh sar- dines were considered for comparison (11.2 g protein per 100 g of pork carcass weight, 16.5 protein per 100 g of beef carcass weight and 18.0 protein per 100 g of fresh weight sardines1 (Bandarra et al., 2004; FAO, 2012b).
Identical system boundaries were taken into account for the three meat production systems studied (i.e., cradle to

slaughterhouse gate), excluding the packaging process. Concerning the fish, system boundaries included all fishery activities up to the sardine landing process for sale at Portuguese fish auctions (Almeida et al., 2013; Belo et al., 2013).

Reckman et al. (2012) reported a GWP of 3.6 kg CO2 eq per kg of pork carcass weight, which is slightly higher than our result for chicken (2.46 kg CO2 eq, Table 6). An array of GHG emissions was found for beef production systems, ranging from 17 to 37 kg CO2 eq per kg of beef carcass weight (Cedeberg and Stadig, 2003; Beauchemin et al., 2010). Therefore, these GWP values corre- spond to 5.4 and 22.4 kg CO2 eq per functional unit (168.4 g protein) for pork and beef, respectively. Concerning sardines, Almeida et al. (2013) and Belo et al. (2013) reported a value of GWP ranging from 0.27 to 0.47 kg CO2 eq per kg of fresh sardines, resulting in an average of 0.35 kg CO2 eq per functional unit. Thus, based on GWP alone, the production of broiler chicken should be preferable to that of pork or beef since it emits up to 44% and 86% fewer GHGs throughout the production life cycle. This statement agrees with other studies (Roy et al., 2008; Wiedema et al., 2008). In fact, Roy et al. (2008) reported that beef is the lowest envi- ronmentally friendly meat since the greater feed conversion ratio (the mass of the feed consumed divided by the gain in body mass) of cows results in higher emissions from feed production. On the contrary, sardines should be preferable compared with broiler chicken as a source of proteins in a meal since the GWP related with the sardine production system should be around 88% lower than chicken. However, although the environmental profile should be better, there are some protocols that must be taken into account for fishery products. In contrast to meat, there are pol- icies in place to limit the number of vessels and days at sea in addition to quota restrictions limiting the total allowable catches (TACs). This action, designed to maintain the fishing stock levels at desirable abundance rates, was imposed due to over- exploitation in some fisheries (Vázquez-Rowe and Tyedmers, 2013).

The results from this study can support policy makers and in- dustries in order to identify improvement options focussed on the reduction of the environmental profiles of food production sys- items. Moreover, it could enable consumers to make informed purchasing decisions when confronted with various alternative protein source products in terms of the environmental perfor- mance of those products.

environmental profile ranging from 84% to 99%. This is also due to organic waste management (mostly NH3 and CH4 emissions from handling and storage), which contributes to EP and AP, and poultry bedding production, which contributes mainly to GWP, ADP and CED. Other broiler-chicken studies have reported similar results (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008). A common result in studies including the slaughtering stage (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007) is the low contribution of the slaughterhouse stage to the total impacts. However, special attention must be paid to electricity consumption in the slaughterhouse, which is particularly high for the WWTP, refrigeration, and the production of the electric shock.

Including slaughterhouse activities, the value of GWP in the present study is around 2.46 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per carcass weight. Excluding them, GWP is around 1.62 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per live weight. GWP results reported in the literature (Baumgartner et al., 2008; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008; Wiedema et al., 2008; Williams et al., 2006) vary significantly (Table 6). The differences in these results depend mainly on the country in which the study was performed, data quality (the use of primary or secondary data), co-products/ waste handling, consideration of hatchery chicks and chicken feed composition. The GWP of broiler chicken production in Portugal is below the average of these results.

When focussing only on chicken farm activities (Baumgartner et al., 2008; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2012; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008; Williams et al., 2006), GWP ranges from 1.48 to 2.61 kg CO2 eq kg- per live weight. Studies from Bengtsson and Seddon (2013), Katajajuuri (2007), Pelletier (2008) and Williams et al. (2006) carried out in very different countries have similar GWPs (2.00-2.61 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per live weight), although they all have different system boundaries. One of the main factors responsible for these slight differences in GWP is the consideration of chicken bed production in Bengtsson and Seddon (2013) and Williams et al. (2006) (as in our study). More- over, within the farm activities, Pelletier (2008) considered the use of poultry litter to displace fertilizer production, the poultry fat and the by-product meal production from waste, as well as the avoid- ance of meal production.

Da Silva et al. (2012) reported a 1.48 and 2.19 kg CO2 eq kg-1 live weight for chicken production in Brazil and France, respectively. The main factor responsible for this different result is related to the feed composition and corresponding production, as well as organic waste management.
The GWP in Baumgartner et al. (2008) is considerably higher (almost double) than our results (Table 6). This study considered

the inclusion of infrastructure production and maintenance within the system boundaries, although the contribution is almost negli- gible in this impact category. Differences in data quality and farming practices (e.g., feed composition and consumption, heat requirements and heating sources) could be the reason for the large difference in GHG emissions.

Weidema et al. (2008) reported results corresponding to an average value for Europe. It is the most complete study as the distribution to retailers and consumption was included (3.60 kg CO2 eq kg-1 per carcass weight). However, it is not possible to identify GHG emissions from the individual processes involved due to the lack of information in the report, meaning it could not be compared with our system. However, the contribution of chicken farm related activities to categories such as GWP, AP and EP are significant according to Weidema et al. (2008). According to a re- view of the poultry sector by Bengtsson and Seddon (2013), the GHG emission rate ranges from 2.00 to 5.48 kg CO2 eq kg` per live weight. Again, our result is below this range.

Environmental impacts have been quantified not only for chicken meat production but also for other types of meat such as pork and beef (Reckman et al., 2012; Beauchemin et al., 2010). Quantifying the environmental impacts of food products is important due to society's interest in consuming more environ- mentally friendly produce. Meat is an important source of proteins for humans and differs in protein content depending on the type of meat. Therefore, we compared the total GHG emissions of the most commonly consumed types of meat (i.e., chicken, pork and beef) (European Commission, 2004) and one of the most consumed types of fish in Portugal (sardines) (Cardoso et al., 2013). The consider- ation of fish as a source of protein is important in Portugal since it has a higher average annual per capita seafood consumption than all but one other European country (Cardoso et al., 2013).

To do so, an equivalent functional unit was required. We considered 168.4 g of protein, which corresponds to the amount of protein supplied by one Portuguese broiler chicken with an average weight of 1.20 kg (14.04 g protein per 100 g of carcass weight). Therefore, 1.50 kg of pork, 1.02 kg of beef and 0.94 kg of fresh sar- dines were considered for comparison (11.2 g protein per 100 g of pork carcass weight, 16.5 protein per 100 g of beef carcass weight and 18.0 protein per 100 g of fresh weight sardines1 (Bandarra et al., 2004; FAO, 2012b).
Identical system boundaries were taken into account for the three meat production systems studied (i.e., cradle to

slaughterhouse gate), excluding the packaging process. Concerning the fish, system boundaries included all fishery activities up to the sardine landing process for sale at Portuguese fish auctions (Almeida et al., 2013; Belo et al., 2013).

Reckman et al. (2012) reported a GWP of 3.6 kg CO2 eq per kg of pork carcass weight, which is slightly higher than our result for chicken (2.46 kg CO2 eq, Table 6). An array of GHG emissions was found for beef production systems, ranging from 17 to 37 kg CO2 eq per kg of beef carcass weight (Cedeberg and Stadig, 2003; Beauchemin et al., 2010). Therefore, these GWP values corre- spond to 5.4 and 22.4 kg CO2 eq per functional unit (168.4 g protein) for pork and beef, respectively. Concerning sardines, Almeida et al. (2013) and Belo et al. (2013) reported a value of GWP ranging from 0.27 to 0.47 kg CO2 eq per kg of fresh sardines, resulting in an average of 0.35 kg CO2 eq per functional unit. Thus, based on GWP alone, the production of broiler chicken should be preferable to that of pork or beef since it emits up to 44% and 86% fewer GHGs throughout the production life cycle. This statement agrees with other studies (Roy et al., 2008; Wiedema et al., 2008). In fact, Roy et al. (2008) reported that beef is the lowest envi- ronmentally friendly meat since the greater feed conversion ratio (the mass of the feed consumed divided by the gain in body mass) of cows results in higher emissions from feed production. On the contrary, sardines should be preferable compared with broiler chicken as a source of proteins in a meal since the GWP related with the sardine production system should be around 88% lower than chicken. However, although the environmental profile should be better, there are some protocols that must be taken into account for fishery products. In contrast to meat, there are pol- icies in place to limit the number of vessels and days at sea in addition to quota restrictions limiting the total allowable catches (TACs). This action, designed to maintain the fishing stock levels at desirable abundance rates, was imposed due to over- exploitation in some fisheries (Vázquez-Rowe and Tyedmers, 2013).

The results from this study can support policy makers and in- dustries in order to identify improvement options focussed on the reduction of the environmental profiles of food production sys- items. Moreover, it could enable consumers to make informed purchasing decisions when confronted with various alternative protein source products in terms of the environmental perfor- mance of those products.
24: Improvements can be proposed to decrease environmental impacts, but they will require further research. Due to the signifi- cant contribution to environmental impacts and energy use of feed production as a result of the production of feed ingredients (especially soybean meal), special attention should be paid to this area. Baumgartner et al. (2008) reported that using grain legumes as a protein source in animal feed instead of soybean-based in- gredients could decrease the environmental impacts of animal products, mainly because the cultivation of grain legumes does not require mineral fertilizer application. There are clear benefits related to resource-use driven impacts due to the need for less transport, the reduced incorporation of energy-rich feeds and the absence of land transformation. Baumgartner et al. (2008) also promoted the use of locally produced animal feed based on the lower input rates for crop production and processing, which would thus have lower environmental impacts.

Uncontrolled NH3 emissions from manure handling and storage in chicken farms contributed significantly to EP and AP, as reported by others (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; Katajajuuri, 2007; Williams et al., 2006). Therefore, it is important to establish

suitable mechanisms to reduce these emissions. According to Katajajuuri (2007), the use of heat exchangers could increase ventilation in broiler farms, simultaneously reducing NH3 emis- sions and achieving benefits for the health of chickens.

The application of manure on agricultural land as organic fer- tilizer to replace mineral fertilizers has been considered in this case study as a base scenario, taking into account the avoidance of mineral fertilizer production (Scenario A). This situation involves light reductions in the environmental profiles (Fig. 2). This man- agement option involves not only environmental but also cost benefits for chicken farms (Katajajuuri, 2007).

Composting of poultry litter could be an alternative since it is a current practice being carried out in Portugal. For this reason, the composting of organic waste in the chicken farm instead of its use as organic fertilizer in agricultural lands has been proposed for assessment (Scenario B). Inventory data concerning the composting process has been taken from the ecoinvent database (Doka, 2007). Comparative profiles for both scenarios (Scenario A and Scenario B) are shown in Fig. 5. According to the results, the use of poultry litter in compost production is likely to be the worse option (in environmental terms) in comparison with its use as an organic fertilizer. Increments in environmental burdens regardless of the impact category are obtained mainly due to emissions derived from the composting process (specifically in categories such as AP and EP), as well as high energy requirements.

If the use of poultry litter as organic fertilizer was considered without taking into account within the system boundaries the avoided production of a certain amount of mineral fertilizers (Scenario C), worse results should be obtained in comparison with Scenario A regardless of the category. However, better results should be achieved in comparison with Scenario B in all the cate- gories except EP. In terms of EP, Scenario C is likely to be the worse option for poultry litter management mainly due to diffuse emis- sions into air and water.

28: In Portugal, 75% of chicken meat consumed is broiler chicken and it is the most consumed meat in the country. Several LCA studies involving chicken production have been published to date, but none have focused on the Portuguese chicken sector. For this reason, a representative chicken farm (one of the most important in terms of chicken production volume) and slaugh- terhouse in Portugal were assessed in detail to quantify the environmental and energy profiles of the broiler chicken pro- duction system.

Chicken farm processes were identified as the main contributors to environmental impacts and energy use. Specifically, feed pro- duction and on-farm emissions were detected as the main envi- ronmental hotspots. Concerning the slaughterhouse, the production of packaging materials and electricity requirements played a major role in its environmental profile.

The hotspots observed, as well as the GHG emissions per func- tional unit, were in accordance with those mentioned in other studies. A GWP of approximately 2.46 kg CO2 eq kg` per carcass weight was estimated in this cradle-to-gate study. GWP results reported in the literature from other countries vary significantly, mainly depending on system boundaries, rearing scenarios, the country in which the study was carried out and data quality.

When comparing the GWP of chicken production systems with those of pork and beef production systems, considering a functional unit based on the same protein supply (168.4g), the production of broiler chicken had the lowest GWP. However, an alternative pro- tein source from fish such as sardines (Portugal occupies a leading position in terms of hauls and consumption) presented better re- sults in terms of GHG emissions, with reductions of up to 88% in comparison with chicken. However, the availability of sardines for consumption is limited throughout the year due to fishing protocols.

Numerous studies report that the establishment of suitable mechanisms to remove NH3 emissions from air, the use of chicken manure as organic fertilizer and the changes to feed composition could all help reduce the environmental impacts of chicken pro- duction. However, more research should be conducted in order to guarantee these potential improvements. Composting poultry litter should not be considered as an alternative for agricultural appli- cation as it had worse environmental results in all the categories assessed.

The results of this study are interesting for both LCA practi- tioners and stakeholders in the meat sector since chicken meat occupies the second position in the meat consumption ranking in Europe. Moreover, the results from this study could encourage consumers towards more sustainable consumption in terms of food products.
